actions.thought: It is the first hand of the match, so I have no information on my opponent's tendencies. I am holding J5o on the Button (SB). While heads-up play encourages a wide opening range due to positional advantage, J5o is in the bottom 15-20% of starting hands and is generally considered a fold in standard charts. Playing this hand weak and unconnected hand creates difficult post-flop scenarios. A disciplined fold is the standard play here.

In a heads-up match, the button (Small Blind) should play a wide range of hands. However, Jack-Five offsuit (J5o) is towards the bottom of the playable range. Given it is the beginning of the match and I have no information on the opponent's tendencies, I will play conservatively and fold this marginal hand to avoid unnecessary post-flop complications out of position or in a bloated pot.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit (AcQd) on the Button/Small Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. With 9950 chips and blinds at 50/100, raising is the standard play to build the pot and take the initiative. A minimum raise to 200 allows me to play a balanced strategy, opening a wide range of hands for a cheap price while extracting value with my strong holdings. I have position post-flop, reinforcing the decision to play aggressively.

I hold Ace-Eight suited, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation from the Big Blind. Pro raised to 200 from the Button, which is a standard opening. While I could just call to see a flop, 3-betting (raising) allows me to take the initiative, define the hand strength, and potentially win the pot immediately. A raise to 600 is a standard 3-bet size in this position.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit, a premium hand in heads-up play, and I have position on the button. Facing a 3-bet to 600, folding is incorrect given the strength of my hand. A 4-bet could isolate me against a range that dominates me or flips well (like AK or pairs), whereas calling keeps the opponent's range wide, including dominated Aces and random bluffs. Since we are reasonably deep (starting roughly 100BB), playing a 3-bet pot in position with a strong broadway hand is advantageous. I will call the additional 400 to see a flop.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage on this King-high board. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot (400) allows me to capitalize on this advantage, forcing folds from many of the opponent's weaker holdings and maintaining control of the hand. I have an overcard and a backdoor flush draw as backup equity if called.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit on a K-9-3 rainbow board. After calling a 3-bet pre-flop, I am facing a small continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 1200 (1/3 pot). The board is dry, and while the King hits the 3-bettor's range (AK, KQ), the small sizing suggests a range bet that includes many hands I am ahead of or have equity against (lower pairs, Ax bluffs). I have two overcards, a backdoor nut flush draw (Ac), and position. Given the pot odds (requiring ~20% equity), folding the top of my unpaired range here would be too weak. Floating allows me to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

The Deuce of Spades on the turn is a complete brick and does not change the board texture. After my continuation bet was called on the flop, it is likely that the opponent has a piece of this board, such as a King, a Nine, or a mid-pocket pair. Continuing to barrel with Ace-high into a range that just called a flop bet on such a dry board is risky. Checking here allows me to control the pot size and see how Pro reacts. If the opponent checks back, I might still have the best hand or can potentially bluff on certain river cards. If they bet, I will likely fold given I have no made hand and limited equity.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit on a K-9-3-2 rainbow board. The opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, has now checked the turn. This check likely indicates pot control with a marginal made hand (like an underpair to the King or a weak King) or a give-up with air. My Ace-high has showdown value against their bluffs, and I have 6 outs to improve to top pair. Betting here has little utility: I unlikely fold out better hands (like pairs), and worse hands (pure bluffs) will simply fold, denying me the chance to induce a bluff on the river or win at showdown. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and keeps the pot manageable.

On the river, the board texture remains very dry and the 4s is unlikely to have improved either player's hand. My Ace-high has some showdown value in a heads-up pot, but it is unlikely to be the best hand given that Pro called both a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop. Pro's check back on the turn suggests a marginal made hand like a Nine or a mid-pocket pair, which they are likely looking to take to a cheap showdown. Bluffing into this range is risky because those hands are unlikely to fold to a single river bet after showing interest earlier. Checking is the most prudent play, allowing me to see a showdown for free if Pro checks back, or to fold if Pro bets, as my Ace-high is rarely ahead if the opponent puts more money in the pot now.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit on a K-9-3-2-4 rainbow board. The pot is 2000. My opponent 3-bet pre-flop, c-bet the flop, checked the turn, and has now checked the river. This line strongly suggests either a marginal made hand (like an underpair to the King or mid-pair) trying to get to showdown, or a complete air hand (like AJ/AT or missed suited connectors) giving up. My hand has significant showdown value against the latter portion of his range. Betting here is unlikely to be profitable: I cannot value bet as worse hands will fold, and bluffing is difficult because my check-back on the turn limits the strong hands I can credibly represent. Furthermore, I unlikely fold out small pairs or 9x that beat me. Checking back maximizes EV by realizing my equity against his bluffs without risking chips against his made hands.

I am holding 53 offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. While I could attempt a raise to steal the blinds, 53o is near the bottom of my range and doesn't warrant a call or a raise against a competent opponent in the Big Blind. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am holding 9d 4s on the Button (Small Blind) in a heads-up match. This is a 9-high off-suit hand, which falls into the bottom 10-15% of starting hands. While heads-up requires playing a wide range (often 80%+ of buttons), 94o is generally too weak to profitably open-raise or limp, especially this early in the tournament without strong reads on the opponent's post-flop tendencies. I folded J5o in Hand 1, and this hand is weaker. The standard play is to fold.

I am holding 9c 4s on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very weak hand, even for the wide ranges typically played in heads-up poker. While it is important to apply pressure from the small blind, 94o is near the bottom of my range and doesn't play well post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the early stage of the match, I will choose to fold this hand rather than risking chips with low equity.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with K8o. In heads-up play, this is a decent starting hand, well within the top 50% of hands. My opponent has folded pre-flop a few times, suggesting they might not be defending their Big Blind extremely widely. Raising allows me to take the initiative, capitalize on position post-flop, and potentially take down the pot immediately if the opponent folds. A minimum raise to 200 is standard to apply pressure while minimizing risk.

Holding 92 offsuit in the big blind, I am facing a raise from the button. While the price to call is small (only 100 more into a 300 pot), 92o is one of the weakest hands in poker and performs very poorly post-flop, especially when out of position. Given the long-term nature of this 150-hand match, there is no need to defend such a marginal hand and risk more chips on later streets.

Holding Jh 5h on the button (SB) in a heads-up match, I have a hand with decent playability and potential. Raising is standard here to apply pressure on the Big Blind and gain the initiative in the hand. I will raise to 300, which is 3x the big blind, to either take down the blinds or set up a post-flop play with a suited hand.

I am in the Big Blind with KJo, a strong hand in Heads-Up play. The opponent raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. Facing a 3x open, I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, offering 2:1 odds. KJo has excellent equity against a wide button opening range. While a 3-bet is possible, calling is prudent to keep the opponent's range wide and play a manageable pot Out of Position, especially given the slightly larger pre-flop raise size. I will call to see a flop.

I am out of position on a paired board (7s 7h 2h). My hand (Kc Js) has decent showdown value as King-high, beating missed broadways and lower unconnected cards. Standard heads-up strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they proceed. Leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose here as I won't get called by worse often enough, and I don't want to build a pot with just King-high out of position. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

On a paired board of 2h-7s-7h, I have a heart flush draw and one overcard (the Jack). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and my opponent checked to me, a continuation bet is standard here. I will bet 300, which is 50% of the pot, to maintain initiative and either take the pot down now or build it for when I potentially hit my flush. The paired board makes it less likely that my opponent has a strong hand like a 7.

I hold King-high (Ks Jc) on a paired board of 7s 7h 2h. In a Heads-Up match, King-high has significant showdown value against a button opening range that contains many weaker hands and pure bluffs. The opponent has c-bet 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot). To call, I need 25% equity. Against a wide c-betting range, my hand beats their air (missed connectors, lower broadways) and has 6 outs against pairs (Kings and Jacks), giving me sufficient equity to continue. Folding now would be too tight given the wide ranges involved in HU play. Raising turns my hand into a bluff when I have showdown value. Therefore, calling is the most profitable line to keep their bluffing range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the board reads 2h 7s 7h Ac. The Ace is a significant scare card that interacts heavily with the opponent's pre-flop raising and flop c-betting range. While I hold King-high (Ks Jc), which had decent showdown value on the flop, the Ace downgrades my hand strength significantly relative to the opponent's likely holdings. As the out-of-position player, leading (donk betting) is not viable here; I would only be called by better hands or fold out bluffs I beat. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent checks back, I get to see a free river with King-high. If they bet, I will face a difficult decision but likely have to fold, as calling a double barrel on an Ace turn with just King-high is generally -EV. Therefore, checking is the correct action.

The Ace of clubs on the turn is a significant scare card. As the pre-flop and flop aggressor, this card hits my range very well, and I could easily be holding an Ace. However, Pro has called both a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet, indicating they likely have a piece of the board or a pocket pair. Since I have a flush draw with my Jack-high hearts, I have about an 18% chance of hitting the flush on the river. Checking here allows me to see the river for free and realize my equity without the risk of being check-raised off my draw. If I hit the heart on the river, I can value bet, and if I miss, I can decide whether to bluff or give up based on the final board and Pro's action.

The river is the Ten of Spades, making the board 2h 7s 7h Ac Ts. The flush draw from the flop missed. The opponent checked back the turn when the Ace fell, suggesting a capped range consisting of weak Aces, pot-controlled pairs, or gave-up bluffs. My hand (King-High) holds showdown value against the missed flush draws and lower air. Betting here is unlikely to be profitable as it won't fold out an Ace or a Ten, and likely not even a small pair, essentially turning my hand into a bluff with little leverage. Checking allows me to realize my equity against the opponent's bluffs/air for free. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold, but checking gives the best chance to win the pot without risking more chips.

I missed my flush draw and I am left with only Jack-high. While the Ace on the turn was a good card for my range, my decision to check behind on the turn likely signaled a lack of strength or a 'give-up' to my opponent. Pro called my continuation bet on the flop, which indicates they likely have a small pair or some connection to the board. Given that Pro has checked the river as well, they may have a hand with some showdown value (like a small pocket pair or a Ten) that they are willing to take to a cheap showdown. Since a bluff is unlikely to be successful after my turn check, I will check behind and concede the pot, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

In Heads-Up play, 52s is one of the weakest suited hands in the range (bottom 10-15%). While I have the position (Button/SB) and a stack lead, the opponent (Flash) has shown aggression from the Big Blind by 3-betting in Hand #3. Raising with 5-high risks burning chips against a 3-bet or being dominated post-flop. Limping is a consideration to see a cheap flop, but given the hand's poor playability and lack of connectivity (3-gapper), folding and preserving the stack lead is the disciplined, low-variance play early in the tournament (Hand 9/150).

In heads-up play, Queen-Nine offsuit (Q9o) is a strong starting hand from the button/small blind and is well within the standard opening range. I will raise to 300 (a 3x open) to build the pot and take control of the hand, forcing the Big Blind to either fold or play out of position.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3bb raise (300 total) from the Button. My hand, 6h 3s, is statistically very weak (bottom 10% of hands) and suffers from poor playability post-flop, especially being out of position. Although I get 3:1 pot odds, the equity realization for 63o against a button opening range is too low to justify a call, particularly against a larger size raise like 3x. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve chips.

I hold QJo on the Button (SB), which is a strong starting hand in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em. I have position on the opponent for the rest of the hand. My stack depth is comfortable (over 80 BBs). Raising allows me to take the initiative, build the pot with a good hand, and potentially take down the blinds immediately. A standard min-raise to 200 chips is appropriate to balance my range and offer the opponent a price to call while still asserting aggression.

I am holding King-Nine of diamonds in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Pro has opened for a minimum raise of 2x (200). To take initiative and build a pot with a hand that has significant equity and playability, I will 3-bet to 600. This puts pressure on the button's wide opening range and allows me to define my hand strength while remaining balanced with my previous aggressive play in Hand #3.

I am facing a 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind. I hold QJo on the Button, which is a strong hand in heads-up play, well within the top defending range against a 3-bet. The pot odds are favorable (calling 400 to win a pot of 800, giving me 2:1 odds requiring ~33% equity), and I will have the advantage of position post-flop. 4-betting would likely isolate me against a range that dominates me (AQ, AJ, KQ, KK+), while folding is too tight given the hand strength and match dynamic. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide and leverage my position to navigate post-flop streets.

The flop is 4h-4s-6d, which is a very dry and paired board. As the pre-flop 3-better, I generally have the range advantage here, as my range includes high pairs and strong broadway cards that Pro might have just called with pre-flop. I have King-high with a backdoor diamond flush draw. I will lead with a continuation bet of 400 (1/3 of the pot) to maintain aggression and fold out his air, which is consistent with my play in Hand #3.

I am playing Heads-Up in a 3-bet pot. The board texture is very dry (4-4-6 rainbow), which generally favors the pre-flop aggressor who is more likely to hold overpairs. While the opponent's 1/3 pot c-bet (400 into 1200) is small and offers decent pot odds (20%), my hand (QJo) is near the bottom of my defending range. I have no backdoor flush draws and only two overcards, which might be dominated by hands like AQ, AJ, KQ, or pocket pairs. Without a pair or a draw, and facing aggression on a board that doesn't improve my hand, floating here is a losing play in the long run. I have plenty of other hands (pairs, Ace-highs, suited connectors) to defend with. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

I am on the button with 2c6c, a speculative suited hand. In a heads-up game with 90 big blinds, I want to maintain a high frequency of opening from the button to put pressure on the big blind. Although the hand is weak, its suitedness provides some post-flop playability. I will raise to 300 (a 3x open) to take control of the pot early.

I am in the Big Blind with Q4o facing a 3bb raise (300) from the Button. While Q4o has some raw equity against a wide heads-up range, it plays poorly post-flop due to dominance issues and lack of connectivity. The pot odds (requiring ~33% equity) are worse than against a min-raise, and being out of position makes realizing that equity difficult. Given the opponent's larger sizing, folding is the standard and prudent play.

In heads-up play, T8s is a strong candidate for opening from the Button (Small Blind). It is a suited one-gapper with good post-flop playability. Although the opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to 3-bet against my raises (Hand #3 and #11), I have also folded my button frequently (Hands #1, #5, #9) with trash hands, so my opening range should be perceived as reasonably strong. Raising to 200 allows me to build the pot in position and take the initiative. Folding is too weak, and while limping is an option, raising is the standard aggressive play to maximize EV with this hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Deuce of hearts. My opponent has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. The pot is currently 300, and I need to call 100 more to see a flop. K2s is a hand with some playability due to its suit and high card value, and the pot odds are 3:1. Given the wide range of a button raiser in heads-up play, this is a standard defend. I will call to see a flop rather than 3-betting, as my hand is not strong enough to build a massive pot pre-flop but too strong to fold for one extra blind.

I have hit top pair with King-Deuce of hearts on a board of Jack-King-Eight with two diamonds. While top pair is a strong hand in heads-up play, the board is quite coordinated with several straight and flush draws available, and my kicker is weak. Acting first, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they value this texture. This allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially catch a bluff or evaluate their betting strength.

I hold bottom pair (Eights) on a wet board (Ks Jd 8d) that heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range (which contains many Kx, Jx, and flush draws). While I have position, betting here achieves little: worse hands likely fold, and better hands (Top/Middle pair) will call or check-raise. By checking back, I exercise pot control, realize my equity (including backdoor spade and straight possibilities), and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. This allows me to re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and the opponent's action.

The turn card is the Queen of Diamonds, which is extremely coordinated with the rest of the board (Jd-Ks-8d). This card completes several possible straights (like T9) and the diamond flush. Having checked the flop, I still have top pair with a weak kicker, but the board is now very dangerous. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, as betting into this board is risky given the many combinations that now beat me.

On the turn, the board is Jd Ks 8d Qd. I hold 8s Ts, giving me a pair of Eights and an open-ended straight draw (T-J-Q-K, needing a 9 or A). The Queen of Diamonds is a scary card as it brings a third diamond and connects the broadway cards further. Flash has checked to me again. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, and I might get blown off my equity if check-raised. Checking back allows me to realize my straight equity for free and potentially win at showdown or hit my straight on the river without inflating the pot on a dangerous board.

The river Ace is a very scary card on this board (Jd Ks 8d Qd Ah). My pair of Kings, which was top pair on the flop, is now only the second-best pair. The board also contains three diamonds, making a flush possible, and any Ten makes a straight. Since the action went check-check on both the flop and the turn, I have no reason to believe I am ahead of a range that includes many Aces, Queens, or Jacks that might have checked for pot control. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or to evaluate Pro's response. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands and would fold out hands that I already beat.

The river Ace puts four cards to a Broadway straight (J-Q-K-A) on the board. Holding the Ten of Spades, I have made the best possible straight. While the board contains three diamonds, making a flush possible, the opponent's completely passive line (checking flop, turn, and river) makes a flush highly unlikely, as he would typically bet or check-raise a strong hand or draw earlier. With a four-card straight on the board, it is hard for the opponent to call with just a pair, but checking back guarantees no extra value. A value bet of 200 (half-pot) targets top pair (Ax) or two pair looking to chop, and represents a thin value bet or bluff that might induce a call. If raised, I can comfortably fold crediting the flush.

The board has become extremely coordinated (Jd Ks 8d Qd Ah). Having check-checked the flop and turn, the Ace on the river is a significant scare card. My pair of Kings, which was top pair on the flop, is now weakened by the presence of an Ace, any Ten (which makes a straight), and the three diamonds on board (making a flush possible). Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, checked back twice and is now betting 200 into a 400 pot. While the pot odds are 3:1, my hand is now only the third-best possible pair and loses to a huge portion of Pro's range that might have played passively, such as Ax, any Ten, or any two diamonds. It is unlikely my King is ahead often enough to justify a call on this specific texture.

I am on the button with T8o, which is a standard hand to open or complete in a heads-up situation. The stack sizes are relatively deep (88.5 BB for me, 110 BB for Pro). Pro has shown some aggression pre-flop in previous hands (Hand #3, Hand #11). Against a strong opponent, a 3x open is standard from the button. Raising here allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately if Pro folds.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Threes (3s, 3h) facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. The opponent (Flash) has been raising frequently (3x sizing). We are effectively 86 BB deep, which gives me sufficient implied odds to set-mine. My hand has roughly 50% equity against a wide opening range. Calling is the most standard play here; 3-betting bloats the pot out of position with a hand that plays poorly post-flop unless it improves, and folding is too tight given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 600). I will call and look to hit a set or a favorable low board.

I hold pocket threes (3s 3h) on a board of Js Qd 7c. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which likely contains many broadway cards (Qx, Jx) and higher pairs. I am out of position with a weak underpair that has very low equity and little potential to improve. Leading out (donk betting) would serve no purpose as better hands will call or raise, and worse hands (like pure air) might fold but are few. The standard line is to check to the aggressor. If facing a standard continuation bet, I will likely have to fold given the poor match-up.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding T8o on a Q-J-7 rainbow board. I have a gutshot straight draw to a 9. Pro has checked to me on the flop. This board favors my range as the button opener, and a continuation bet allows me to maintain initiative, represent a strong hand (like a Queen or Jack), and potentially win the pot immediately or build it for when I hit my draw. A 50% pot bet is standard here.

I hold Pocket Threes (3s, 3h) on a Flop of Js-Qd-7c. This board texture hits the pre-flop aggressor's range very hard (Queens, Jacks, straight draws like T9, K10). I have an underpair to the board with virtually no equity to improve and no backdoor draws. Facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600), my hand is too weak to continue. I am drawing to 2 outs at best, and often drawing dead or nearly dead. Calling out of position is losing strategy here. The disciplined play is to fold.

I hold 3c 9h in the Small Blind (Button). This is a very weak hand (9-3 offsuit), ranking in the bottom 15% of starting hands. In Heads-Up play, while ranges are wider, this specific hand usually falls into the folding range, especially against an opponent like Flash who has demonstrated aggression (frequent raises and 3-bets) pre-flop. Playing this hand, even with position post-flop, often leads to negative EV situations or being forced to fold to a 3-bet. Therefore, open-folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

K7o is a solid hand for heads-up play on the Button. Pro has been folding to many of my pre-flop raises recently, so I will continue to apply pressure with a standard 3x open raise to 300. This allows me to take control of the hand or win the pot immediately if Pro folds.

My opponent has raised to 300 (3bb) from the button. This is a larger sizing than a min-raise, which worsens my pot odds (requiring ~33% equity to call). I hold K3o in the Big Blind. While heads-up requires wide defense, K3o is difficult to play out of position, especially against a 3x open. My opponent is not opening 100% of buttons (having folded in hands 2, 4, and 6), suggesting their range is reasonably strong (likely top 60-70%). Against this range, K3o has decent raw equity but suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds. I have folded similar weak hands (Q4o, 36o) to this sizing previously. I will fold.

The opponent (Flash) has shown a very high 3-bet frequency so far (3-betting 2 out of the 4 times I have raised from the Button). 95s is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet and is dominated by many calling hands. While 95s is often an open in a vacuum in Heads-Up play, the opponent's aggressive tendencies make opening the bottom of my range negative EV. I choose to fold to preserve chips and wait for a hand that can better withstand aggression.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While this hand has some high card value in a heads-up match, it is generally at the bottom of a playable opening range. Looking at my previous play, I have folded similar or slightly better hands like Jack-Five offsuit and Nine-Four suited. Given that I want to maintain a solid range and not over-commit with trash hands just because I am in position, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am on the Button with K5o, which is a standard opening hand in heads-up poker (top ~44% of hands). While my kicker is weak, I have position throughout the hand. The opponent has shown some aggression (3-betting in Hands 3 and 11), but also flat-called in Hand 13. I cannot fold King-high simply out of fear of a 3-bet, as that would make me too exploitable. A min-raise to 200 attempts to steal the pot immediately or allows me to play in position against a calling range.

I am in the big blind with 8c 6s. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the button/small blind. This is a 2x raise, and I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300. Given the pot odds of 3:1, 86o is a reasonable hand to defend in a heads-up situation, although it's on the weaker side. I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

I have top pair (sixes) on a monotone board of diamonds. This is a very vulnerable position since any diamond from my opponent puts me behind. However, in a heads-up pot, top pair is usually worth a check-call. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they play the board.

I hold middle pair (5s) with a King kicker on a monotone board (5d 2d 6d). Crucially, I do not hold a diamond. This is a very wet texture where my hand has showdown value but is vulnerable. Betting (c-betting) here is often a mistake because I will generally only be called by better hands (better pairs, two pair, sets, flushes) or strong draws (Ace of diamonds), while worse hands that I beat will simply fold. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid getting check-raised, and look to realize my equity by seeing a potentially cheap showdown. I can re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and the opponent's action.

I have improved to top two pair (Eights and Sixes) on the turn. The board shows three diamonds, but my opponent checked back on the flop, which often indicates they do not have a made flush or a high diamond. I will bet 300 into the 400 pot to extract value from one-pair hands or a single diamond draw while protecting my hand from a fourth diamond on the river.

I hold K5o on a 5d-2d-6d-8h board. I checked back the flop with middle pair, and now the opponent leads for 300 into a 400 pot (75% pot) on the turn. The 8h is an overcard to my 5 and the 6, relegating my hand to 3rd pair. The board is very wet with possible flushes and straights. The large bet sizing indicates either a made hand (like an 8, a straight, or a flush) or a strong semi-bluff (like a high diamond draw). Since I hold no diamond and am beating very little of their value range, and facing a large bet, my hand is too weak to continue. I have many better hands in my range to defend (like 8x, Ax with a diamond, or better pairs).

In this heads-up situation, I am on the button with Q2s. While Q2o is a marginal hand, I have the advantage of position throughout the rest of the hand. Standard heads-up play dictates opening a wide range from the small blind/button. A 3x open (300 total) is a standard sizing to put pressure on the big blind and try to take the blinds down immediately or play a pot in position. I have 95 BBs, so there is plenty of room for post-flop play.

I am in the Big Blind with T9o facing a 3x open (300 chips) from the Button. The opponent, Flash, has been opening frequently, suggesting a wide range. I am getting 2:1 pot odds on a call (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring about 33% equity. T9o has approximately 45% equity against a standard wide heads-up opening range. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the hand has decent playability and connectivity. Folding would be too tight in a heads-up match, and 3-betting is not optimal with this specific off-suit connector. Therefore, calling is the most profitable play.

I am out of position on a low, rainbow flop of 5-2-4 with Ten-high and no immediate draws. The board texture connects somewhat with a Big Blind defense range (which includes many low cards), but my specific holding (T9o) has missed completely. Leading out (donk betting) is not advisable as I have no equity to semi-bluff with and little showdown value. The standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold, as floating with just two overcards on this texture is unprofitable.

I have bottom pair with a Queen kicker on a relatively low board of 5-2-4. Having been the pre-flop aggressor and being in position, I will place a continuation bet. This bet serves to maintain initiative, capitalize on any fold equity against high-card hands that missed the flop, and extract value from worse hands or draws. A sizing of 300 (half the pot) is standard for this texture.

I hold Ten-high (T9o) on a low, rainbow flop of 5-2-4. I have no pair, no immediate draw, and practically no backdoor equity other than two overcards. The opponent has c-bet half the pot (300 into 600). While this board texture favors the Big Blind's range generally, it misses my specific hand completely. Calling with just Ten-high out of position against a half-pot bet is unprofitable, as I would need roughly 25% realized equity, which I likely do not have against a c-betting range that includes pairs, sets, straights, and better high cards. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I hold 9d 2h, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While playing heads-up on the button allows for a very wide opening range (often 70-80% or more), 9-2 offsuit falls into the bottom tier of hands that are generally unprofitable to play. My opponent, Flash, has shown aggression with 3-bets in previous hands, making a steal attempt with this hand risky. Folding prevents unnecessary chip loss with a hand that has poor playability and very low equity.

In heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is crucial to capitalize on positional advantage. I have been consistently raising to 300 (3x the big blind) with a wide variety of hands to put pressure on the opponent. T6o is a reasonable hand to continue this strategy, as it allows me to take control of the pot early and potentially win pre-flop or outplay the opponent post-flop with the benefit of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 5d, facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button (Flash). My hand is a weak off-suit one-gapper (75o). The pot odds require me to call 200 to win a total pot of 600, needing roughly 33% equity. While 75o might have raw equity close to that against a very wide range, my equity realization will be poor playing out of position against an aggressive opponent who frequently bets post-flop. Given the 3bb sizing rather than a min-raise, folding is the disciplined and correct choice.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with J7o. In heads-up play, this position requires a wide opening range (typically 80-100% of hands) to prevent the opponent from automatically profiting by over-folding. Although I have folded several buttons recently (holding very weak hands like 92o, 93o, 52s), J7o is stronger and falls within a standard opening range. My opponent (Flash) has defended their Big Blind frequently (4 out of 5 times faced with a raise), including two 3-bets. While this aggression is a concern, folding J7o would be too passive and exploitable. I will min-raise to 200 to maximize my positional advantage post-flop and steal the blind if Flash folds. If faced with a 3-bet, I can easily fold this marginal hand.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. The opponent raised to 200 from the Button. Given the strength of my hand and the deep stacks (approximately 100bb), I should 3-bet for value and to gain initiative. I've previously used a 600 sizing for 3-bets in hands like #3 and #11, which is a standard response to a min-raise while out of position.

I opened J7o from the button, which is a standard steal attempt in heads-up play, but not a hand strong enough to withstand significant aggression. My opponent has 3-bet to 600. J7o is easily dominated by a 3-betting range (e.g., AJ, KJ, QJ, 77+), and despite having position post-flop, the hand plays poorly in inflated pots. The pot odds require ~33% equity, which I likely do not realize well enough against a strong range. As noted in my pre-flop plan, this is a clear fold facing a re-raise. I will preserve my stack of 9600 for better spots.

I am on the button with 6s2h, which is a very weak hand. Given that I am in a heads-up match and the blinds are 50/100, the pot is currently 150 and it costs me 50 to call. However, 62o has very poor playability and equity. In the previous hands, the opponent (Pro) has been fairly aggressive and has folded several times when I've applied pressure or when they have had a weak position. However, since my hand is one of the worst in the deck, I'll choose to fold and wait for a better spot rather than limp-folding or trying to bluff with air from out of position later.

I hold 2c 6c on the Button (SB), which is a very weak hand (bottom 15-20% of range). My opponent 'Flash' has shown significant aggression from the Big Blind, 3-betting my button raises frequently (e.g., Hands 3, 11, 23). I am on the button with 2s 4s. While being suited gives it some playability, it is a very weak hand. Looking at my history in this match, I have been folding the bottom portion of my range from the small blind (like 5-3 offsuit and 6-2 offsuit). 2-4 suited is marginally better but still sits at the bottom of the opening range. Against a professional player, I prefer to fold this junk and wait for better spots rather than bloating a pot with a hand that is easily dominated.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with a very weak hand, Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o). In heads-up play, while I have position post-flop, this hand is near the bottom of my range. My opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression by 3-betting pre-flop in several previous hands (e.g., Hand #3, #11, #23). Opening with such a weak hand invites a 3-bet or puts me in a marginal spot even if called. Preserving chips is better than engaging with trash hands against an aggressive opponent.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, especially from the Small Blind/Button position. My opponent, Pro, has been playing relatively tight and often folding to pre-flop pressure or folding post-flop. I will raise to 300 (an additional 250 on top of my 50 small blind) to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a continuation bet on the flop.

I hold Pocket Aces [Ad, Ac] in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. Flash has shown a tendency to open frequently (300 sizing) and play aggressively post-flop (e.g., Hand #8). While a 3-bet is a standard value play, calling is strategically superior here for several reasons: 1) It disguises the strength of my hand, protecting my calling range; 2) It keeps Flash's wide opening range in the pot, whereas a 3-bet might fold out weaker hands I want to dominate; 3) It leverages Flash's aggression, inviting a continuation bet on the flop which I can then check-raise or call down. Given we are deep-stacked (~97BB), I prefer to trap and play a disguised pot out of position rather than bloating the pot immediately and defining my hand strength.

I am out of position with an overpair (AA) on a wet board [Jh, Th, 2c]. After flat-calling the pre-flop raise to trap, standard strategy dictates checking to the initial raiser. Flash has shown a high frequency of c-betting (e.g., Hands #8, #11, #14), and this board texture connects well with their opening range (broadways, suited connectors). Checking allows me to induce a bet, which I can then decide to check-raise for value and protection against flush/straight draws, or check-call to keep their range wide. Leading out would likely fold out their air and deny them the opportunity to bluff.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high on a Jack-high board. While the board is somewhat connected with straight and flush possibilities, my opponent checked to me. I will follow through with a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain initiative and pressure hands like smaller pairs or missed draws to fold. This allows me to take down the pot now or see how my opponent reacts.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ad, Ac) on a wet board of [Jh, Th, 2c]. After trapping pre-flop with a call, I checked the flop and faced a standard c-bet of 300 from Flash. The board texture is coordinated with flush and straight draws (hearts, KQ, 89), which connects well with the opponent's button opening range. While calling would keep his bluffs in, the board is too dynamic to allow free cards or cheap realizations of equity. I need to extract value from top pairs (KJ, QJ, AJ) and charge the draws heavily while I am ahead. A check-raise here defines the hand and builds the pot while I have the likely best hand, serving both for value and protection. I will raise to 1000, roughly 3.3x his bet, to put pressure on draws.

Pro's check-raise on this Jack-Ten-Deuce flop is a very strong move. This board is quite coordinated, and a check-raise often represents a strong hand like top pair, two pair, or a powerful draw. My Ace-high has very little equity against his likely range, and I do not have a strong enough draw to justify calling the additional 700 chips. Since I am behind most of his value range and even some of his bluffs/draws have significant equity against me, folding is the most prudent play to protect my chip lead.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with King-Seven offsuit (K7o). In heads-up play, a King is a strong blocker and K7o has approximately 54% equity against a random hand, making it well worth playing. Folding would be too passive given the hand strength. I have established a pattern of min-raising (to 200) with playable hands like K8o, K5s, and QJo. Raising puts pressure on the Big Blind, potentially taking the pot down pre-flop or building a pot where I have a positional advantage. Therefore, I will raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 200, which is a standard min-raise. Queen-Ten is a very strong hand in heads-up play and is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call (100 more to win a pot that will be 400). While I could 3-bet to take the initiative, calling allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot controlled with a solid broadway hand. I will call the 100 chips to see the flop.

I have missed the flop with Queen-Ten offsuit on a Jack-high board. As the out-of-position player who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will check and see how Pro reacts.

The flop is J-5-6 rainbow. I have King-high (Ks 7c) in position. This board texture hits the Big Blind's calling range reasonably well (Jx, 6x, 5x, straight draws like 43, 78). Betting here with King-high yields little value; worse hands (like Q-high) fold, and better hands (pairs) call or raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (seeing the turn for free), potentially hit a King, or use my position to bluff catch or bluff later if the opponent shows weakness. It also controls the pot size with a marginal hand. In Hand #19, I checked back top pair on a similar low board, establishing a checking range, so checking K-high here balances that range effectively.

I currently hold Queen-Ten offsuit. The flop of Jack-Five-Six went check-check after I called a pre-flop raise, which suggests that Pro might not have a strong hand or a Jack. The Eight of diamonds on the turn gives me a gutshot straight draw (8-9-T-J-Q). Since I am out of position and the pot is small, I will check again. This allows me to see if Pro will check back again, giving me a free card to hit my straight or potentially win at showdown with Queen-high if the hand stays passive, or I can evaluate a bet if Pro decides to lead.

I checked back the flop with King-high. The turn 8d significantly improves my hand, giving me an Open-Ended Straight Draw (holding 7 with board 5-6-8, needing a 4 or 9) in addition to my King overcard out. While a semi-bluff bet is an option to fold out better hands like Ace-high or small pairs, checking back is a very strong play here. It guarantees I see the river card for free to try and hit my straight or King (realizing 100% of my equity) without risk of being check-raised off the hand. If I hit my straight on the river, my hand will be well-disguised for a value bet. Checking also controls the pot size with a drawing hand.

The board has run out Jack-Five-Six-Eight-Ace. I have Queen-high. Pro checked back on both the flop and the turn after raising pre-flop, which suggests they either have a weak made hand (like a small pair) or have given up with air. The Ace on the river is a significant card; it's a good bluffing card because it's in my defending range, but Pro could also have easily checked back an Ace-high hand that just improved. Since I have no showdown value and the pot is small, I will check and see if Pro checks back. If Pro bets, I will likely fold as Queen-high is rarely the best hand here.

I arrived at the river with King-high after checking back the flop and turn. The board is J-5-6-8-A. My hand has very little showdown value, as I lose to any pair and even Ace-high. The opponent (Flash) has shown weakness by checking three streets. The Ace on the river is a perfect scare card that favors my range as the pre-flop raiser. In a very similar situation in Hand #13 (checked down to an Ace river), I bet and Flash folded. Therefore, I perceive high fold equity here. I will turn my hand into a bluff and represent the Ace to force a fold from weak pairs (5x, 6x, 8x) or better high cards.

I am holding Queen-high on a board of Jack-Five-Six-Eight-Ace. After both players checked the flop and turn, Pro has bet 200 into a 400-chip pot when the Ace arrived on the river. While Pro's checks on the previous streets might suggest a weak hand, the Ace is a very strong card for a pre-flop raiser's range. My Queen-high has no showdown value, and calling here would require me to believe Pro is bluffing with a hand weaker than Queen-high, which is unlikely given the betting line. Since I cannot win at showdown and the pot size does not justify a high-risk bluff-raise, I will fold.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings, the second-strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My objective is to build a large pot for value. Since I am on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up match, I will put in a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300 total) to induce a call or a 3-bet from the opponent.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 6d facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. My opponent 'Flash' has been opening frequently. 7d 6d is a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop. I need to call 200 to win a pot of 600, offering me 33% pot odds. My equity against a wide heads-up opening range is significantly higher than this. While a 3-bet is possible to polarize my range, calling is the standard and most robust play to realize equity and navigate post-flop, keeping the opponent's range wide.

I hold bottom pair (7s) on an Ace-high board (Ad 7s 9s). Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser. The board texture favors the opponent's range (which includes many Ax hands). Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands or strong draws. By checking, I allow the opponent to c-bet with bluffs or check back. I have enough showdown value to call a small bet, but for now, checking is the correct first move.

I have Pocket Kings on an Ace-high board. While KK is a very strong hand pre-flop, the presence of the Ace on the board significantly decreases its relative strength. Pro checked, and by checking back, I can control the size of the pot and avoid being check-raised. This also allows me to keep Pro's range wide and potentially see a safe turn card without inflating the pot while I am likely behind any Ace.

The turn card is the Eight of Spades (8s), making the board [Ad 7s 9s 8s]. This card brings a possible flush (three spades on board) and connects the board heavily (6-7-8-9 straight possibilities). I hold [7d 6d], giving me a pair of Sevens and an open-ended straight draw (needing a 5 or a 10). However, I have no spades. The opponent checked back the flop, which usually signals weakness or pot control (marginal pair or weak Ace), though a slow-played monster is possible. Leading out here (donk betting) is risky because if I get raised, I likely have to fold my equity. Checking is the standard line out of position to see a free river or evaluate a bet size. If the opponent bets, I have a pair plus a draw, which may warrant a call depending on the price, but leading offers little advantage on this scary board.

I have a pair of Kings and the second-nut flush draw with the King of Spades. The board is quite dangerous with an Ace and three spades present. Pro has checked twice, which could indicate a weak hand or a trap with an Ace or a made flush. By checking back here, I control the pot size and ensure I see the river for free. If a spade falls, I will have a very strong hand to value bet. If not, I may still have the best hand at showdown or can evaluate based on Pro's river action.

On the River, the board is [Ad 7s 9s 8s 8h]. I hold [7d 6d] for two pair, Eights and Sevens, with an Ace kicker. The opponent raised pre-flop but checked back both the Flop and Turn. This line strongly suggests they do not have a strong hand like a flush, straight, or strong top pair, as they would likely have bet for value or protection on such a wet board. Their range is capped to weak Aces, marginal pairs, or air. While I have showdown value, it is thin; betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like K-high or pocket pairs < 7) and will likely only be called by better hands (Ax, 9x, or an 8). Therefore, betting has little value. Checking allows me to realize my equity at showdown without risking more chips, or potentially induce a bluff from missed draws which I can evaluate. I will check to see the showdown.

I have pocket Kings on a board of Ad-7s-9s-8s-8h. While I have two pair (Kings and Eights), the board is extremely coordinated. There is an Ace present, three spades making a flush possible, and various straight possibilities (any 6, or T-J). My opponent, Pro, has checked every street including the river. While this could indicate weakness, there is very little value in betting here; a hand that I beat (like a 9 or a 7) is unlikely to call a bet on this board, whereas any Ace or better will certainly call or raise. Checking back allows me to take my showdown value and win the pot if my Kings are still the best hand.

In Heads-Up play, Q8o is a respectable starting hand with above-average equity (~55%) against a random hand. I am in the Small Blind (Button) and have position for the rest of the hand. My opponent 'Flash' has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind by calling min-raises (Hands #13, #19, #29), though they have occasionally 3-bet (Hands #3, #23). If called, I can play post-flop with position.

I am in the Big Blind holding JTo. Pro has opened for a min-raise to 200. JTo is a very strong hand to defend in this position as it has good connectivity and high card value. The pot odds are 2:1 (100 to call into a pot of 300), which makes it a clear call rather than a fold or a 3-bet, allowing me to see a flop and utilize the hand's playability.

I have a gutshot straight draw on a board that significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (containing many Ax and Qx hands). Checking to the raiser is the standard and most prudent play here to see how they react and to potentially realize my equity cheaply or fold if the bet is too large.

I hold middle pair (Queens) on an Ace-high board (As Qs 4h). While I have a decent made hand, the Ace hits the opponent's defending range heavily. Betting isolates me against better hands (Ax) or strong draws, while checking back controls the pot size and allows me to realize my equity. I want to get to showdown cheaply rather than building a large pot with a marginal hand on a wet board where I could face a check-raise.

I currently hold Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw to the King. The flop went check-check, which might indicate that Pro is pot-controlling a marginal hand or has missed entirely. The 5h on the turn doesn't change the board texture significantly. I will check to Pro to see if they continue to show passivity. If Pro checks back again, I may be able to see a free river card to hit my straight or potentially take the pot away on the river if the board remains favorable.

I hold middle pair (Queens) and picked up an 8-high flush draw on the turn. The board contains an Ace, which favors the Big Blind's defending range. After checking back the flop, checking the turn again is the prudent play for pot control. It allows me to realize my equity (pair + draw) without facing a potential check-raise that would force me to fold a hand with significant showdown value. I aim to reach the river cheaply.

I have Jack-high and no showdown value after the board paired Queens on the river. Since the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked both the flop and the turn, their range is likely capped and lacks a strong Ace or a Queen. By betting 200 into the 400 pot, I am representing a Queen or a marginal Ace that was played passively for pot control. This bet size is intended to pressure King-high hands and weak pocket pairs to fold, allowing me to take down the pot without a showdown.

I hold Trip Queens (Qh 8s on As Qs 4h 5h Qc board). The flop and turn checked through, under-representing my hand. The opponent leads for half-pot on the river. Given my mediocre kicker (8), I am beaten by many Queens (Q9, QT, QJ, QK, AQ) and beat by fewer (Q2, Q3, Q6, Q7). The opponent could also be value betting an Ace or bluffing a missed draw. Raising isolates me against better hands, while calling captures value from bluffs and occasionally splits or beats worse value bets while losing the minimum to better Queens.
I am on the button with Jack-Nine suited, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My strategy is to raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with T6o facing a 3bb open-raise (300) from the Button. My opponent has consistently opened to 3x. T6o is a weak hand with poor post-flop playability, especially out of position. Defending T6o against a 3x raise is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with [6c 9s], a weak offsuit hand (bottom ~30% of hands). Standard heads-up strategy dictates folding this hand pre-flop as it lacks playability and is negative EV to open-raise or limp against a reasonably active opponent like Flash, who has shown willingness to 3-bet and defend. Preserving the chip lead by avoiding marginal spots with trash hands is prudent.

I am on the Button with Jack-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak hand even in heads-up play. While I have been successful with 3x raises from the Button recently, J2o is near the bottom of my range and doesn't play well post-flop if called. Given the stack depths and the number of hands remaining, there is no need to force a play with such a weak hand. I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 5s 7c, a weak unsuited one-gapper. In previous hands, I have consistently folded weak hands like 5-J, 9-4, 2-5, etc., from this position. I am holding pocket sevens, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard procedure from the small blind is to open with a raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. I have been consistently raising to 300 in this position throughout the match, and I will continue that sizing here to maintain balance and value.

I hold a pocket pair of threes (3c, 3d) in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. In a heads-up scenario, any pocket pair has reasonable equity against a wide opening range. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 200 chips to play for a pot that will be 600 total, offering 3:1 odds. While 33 is difficult to play post-flop out of position due to the likelihood of overcards, it is strong enough to defend the Big Blind. 3-betting is risky as it inflates the pot with a hand that has poor playability when called. Therefore, calling to set-mine or bluff-catch on favorable textures is the best decision.

I hold pocket threes (3c, 3d) on a flop of 5d-Ah-9s. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture (A-high) heavily favors my opponent's range. My hand is currently an underpair to the board with minimal showdown value and only two outs to improve to a set. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands or fold out worse ones that I might beat at showdown if they check back. The standard play is to check to the raiser. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold given my weak relative strength. If they check back, I can re-evaluate on the turn.

I have pocket sevens on an A-9-5 rainbow flop. While sevens are a strong pre-flop hand, the Ace is a scary card that hit the opponent's range frequently. The opponent checked to me, which could be a trap with an Ace or simply a weak hand. I will bet 300 (half-pot) as a continuation bet to test where I stand. If I am called, I will likely slow down on later streets unless a seven or a clean card arrives. This bet also protects my hand against overcards like KQ or KJ.

I hold pocket threes (3c, 3d) on a flop of 5d-Ah-9s. I checked and my opponent bet 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot). This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor who has all the strong Aces in their range. My hand is an underpair and significantly behind any Ax, 9x, or pocket pair my opponent holds. While my opponent could be bluffing, I am out of position with a very weak hand that will struggle to realize equity on future streets. I have many better hands in my range to defend with (like any Ace, Nine, Five, or gutshot draws). Calling here would be loose and likely unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the disciplined decision.

I am on the Button with Ks8h. In heads-up play, K8o is an above-average hand with good high-card strength. I have established a pattern of min-raising to 200 from the button, which has elicited folds or calls from this opponent previously (e.g., Hand #7 with the same cards). Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or play in position with a range advantage. Folding is too passive for this holding, and limping allows the BB to realize equity for free or raise me.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Deuce offsuit. The opponent has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Given that this is a heads-up situation and I only need to commit 100 more chips to see a flop in a pot that will be 400, calling is a standard and profitable play with any Ace. I have seen the opponent raise to this sizing frequently, and I want to see if I can hit an Ace or a favorable board before committing more chips.

I am the Big Blind and it is my turn to act first on the flop. The board is 5d-5c-8c, and I have Ace-high. This board doesn't hit my range particularly hard, nor does it necessarily favor the pre-flop aggressor, though they could easily have an 8 or a pocket pair. Checking is the standard play here to see how the opponent reacts. Leading out (donking) would likely only get called by better hands and doesn't provide much utility for my specific hand.

I hold Top Pair (Eights) with a strong King kicker on a paired board (5-5-8) that also presents a flush draw (clubs). In a previous similar situation (Hand #19), I checked back top pair and was forced to fold to a turn lead. To avoid giving a free card to flush draws or overcards, and to extract value from worse pairs (66, 77) or draws, I will make a standard continuation bet of half the pot (200). This protects my hand and builds the pot while I am likely ahead.

On a paired board like 5d-5c-8c, a continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor is very common and doesn't necessarily indicate a strong hand. I have Ace-high, which still possesses significant showdown value in a heads-up pot. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 800), meaning I only need about 25% equity to make this a profitable call. Given the range of hands the opponent could be betting here—including overcards, straight draws, or flush draws—Ace-high is often ahead or has enough equity to see a turn card. I will call to see how the action develops on the next street.

I am out of position on a paired board of 5d-5c-8c-7s. I called a continuation bet on the flop with Ace-high, which still holds some showdown value but is vulnerable. Checking is the most appropriate action here to control the size of the pot and see how the opponent reacts. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor after calling the flop doesn't achieve much, as I'm likely only called by better hands or draws that have significant equity against me. I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action.

I hold Top Pair (Eights) with a King kicker. After my pre-flop raise and flop c-bet were called, the turn brings a 7s. The board is [5d, 5c, 8c, 7s]. This turn card completes a straight for 6-9 and connects with hands like 87 or 76 that might have called the flop. While I am still ahead of flush draws and worse 8s (like T8, 98), betting here opens me up to a check-raise from trips (5x) or a completed straight, and might fold out hands I'm beating. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, avoid a difficult spot against a check-raise, and induce bluffs on the river from missed draws.
